title: Traditional braided rag rug
subtitle: A coiled oval rug braided from fabric strips
excerpt: Cut old fabrics into strips, braid them into a long rope, then coil and stitch the rope into an oval rug. The finished rug is about 60 cm by 90 cm and requires no special equipment. Braided rag rugs are among the oldest thrift-craft projects in British and American household tradition.
sourceNotes: Braided rag rug technique documented across multiple public-domain American and British household craft manuals including Needlecraft Magazine volumes (1910s-1920s, public domain, Internet Archive). The coiled oval construction is the standard British and North American domestic method.

glossaryTerms.definition: Fabric cut into long narrow strips to form the braiding material. Strips cut 2 to 3 cm wide from medium-weight cotton, denim, or wool fabric give a firm, durable braid that holds its shape in the finished rug.
In rag rug-making, the tightening of the coiled braid by pulling each new coil firmly against the previous one so the rug lies flat without gaps.

content.text: A braided rag rug converts old fabric directly into a durable floor covering with no special tools beyond a needle and scissors. Old cotton shirts, worn denim, wool blankets, and curtain fabric all work well. The three-strand braid is the standard construction; the strips from three different fabrics produce the characteristic variegated surface of a traditional British or New England rag rug.

items.name: Fabric strips, 2 to 3 cm wide, in three or more colours
items.qty: 5 kg total for a 60 x 90 cm rug

items.name: Strong upholstery thread or linen thread
Large blunt needle (curved upholstery needle preferred)

content.text: Cut or tear the fabric into strips 2 to 3 cm wide across the grain. For a tidy braid, fold the raw edges of each strip inward as you work: fold each long edge to meet at the centre, then fold in half again to hide all raw edges. Alternatively, for thicker fabrics like denim, work with the strips unfolded; the raw edges will be hidden inside the braid.

Tie three strips together at one end in an overhand knot. Secure the knot end to a firm surface with a clip or hook. Braid the three strips in a standard over-under-over pattern, keeping consistent tension. Add new strips by folding the new strip around the old and continuing; the join is hidden inside the braid. Continue until the braid is several metres long.
Step 3: Coil and stitch the oval

For an oval rug, begin by placing about 30 cm of braid flat and folding the start back on itself. Stitch the two sides of the fold together firmly using the upholstery thread. This creates the oval starting point. Continue adding the braid in a coil around the starting piece, stitching each new loop to the previous one using a firm, flat stitch through the edges of adjacent braid rows. Do not pull the stitches tight; the coil should lie flat, not pucker.

When the rug has reached the desired size, taper the final 20 cm of braid by progressively folding the strips narrower as you braid the final section. Tuck the tapered end under the adjacent braid row and stitch firmly in place so the end is invisible from above. The
form a self-finished edge. Check that the rug lies flat; push any raised areas flat while the rug is still flexible and the stitches are not yet set. The key to a flat rug is consistent
of each new coil row as it is stitched.

items.problem: The rug bowls upward in the centre
items.cause: The braid was pulled too tight as the coils were stitched, or not enough braid was added per coil revolution
items.fix: Pull the rug gently over the back of a chair to flatten it, or press it under heavy books overnight. On the next coil, add slightly more braid length per revolution to let the coil sit flat.

items.problem: The stitching keeps breaking after the rug is walked on
items.cause: Thread weight is too fine for the stresses of floor use
items.fix: Use waxed linen thread or upholstery thread rather than standard sewing thread. Re-stitch any broken areas with thicker thread and a short backstitch rather than a running stitch.
